sections.title: Standard Sales and Operations Planning
sections.text:
11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning
The following sections give an overview of S&OP and the similarities and differences
between standard S&OP and flexible planning. They then move on to cover forecast-
ing, and they conclude with coverage of standard analysis in flexible planning.

sections.title: Overview
sections.text:
11.1.1 Overview
In standard S&OP, a planner can plan individual materials or a group of materials
known as a product group. A product group consists of individual materials or other
product groups, and it enables a planner to define the proportion factor (percentage) for
each material in the overall product group. It also offers the option to aggregate and dis-
aggregate at various planning levels. If the planning processes in a company are rela-
tively simple and straightforward—that is, if they are restricted to individual materials
or a group of materials—then standard S&OP can fulfill the company’s business needs.
Example
Consider the following business scenario: Before each financial year begins, the sales
and production teams spend countless hours working on planning figures that are
acceptable to both. The sales team uses forecasting tools to arrive at next year’s sales
figures (targets), based on historical data. The production team looks at things differ-
ently. They evaluate whether they are able to meet the demand for sales with existing
production capacities or not. The procurement planner needs a better understanding
of how the production figures will impact the procurement process: do the vendors
even have the capabilities to meet the supply requirements of the company? The
inventory controller (of the warehouse) is concerned about whether there is enough
space in the warehouse to manage and store the produced quantities. The manage-
ment of the company is interested in not only increasing the profitability of the prod-
uct (and at the same time, reducing costs) but also gaining a broader understanding of
the capital tie-up involved and whether new investment (e.g., capacity enhancement,
increasing the number of working shifts) is warranted. Furthermore, knowing which
products (or groups of products), regions, or markets can bring in greater revenue for
the company also helps in the decision-making process.
This is where S&OP can help. In general, the entire planning exercise entails the cre-
ation of various planning versions in the system and adjusting sales or production fig-
ures until there is a mutual consensus. The finalized planning figures, which usually
reflect a production plan, are sent forward to demand management, where they
appear as planned PIRs with an active version. When the system runs the MRP, it con-
siders the active PIRs to arrive at procurement proposals (for in-house production and
external procurement).
In the planning process, the teams need a planning table in the SAP S/4HANA system
that can take all of the important planning considerations into account and at the
same time account for the dependencies of various factors on others.
© 

547
11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning
Flexible planning functionality, while very sophisticated, is also a tool that is slightly
complex to manage. But if a planner can gain a better understanding of flexible plan-
ning, they can reap higher dividends from it, and it will help them produce much more
realistic planning figures, which their company can use to reap greater financial bene-
fits. For example, with flexible planning, a planner can configure a planning layout
(known as a planning table) of important key figures; include self-defined macros to
manage complex calculations; perform forecasting (which is also possible in standard
S&OP); take into account special events (e.g., trade shows, the Olympics) for increases
in sales or take into account natural calamities (e.g., droughts, floods) to factor in
decreases in sales (or increases, if their company manufactures relevant products); and
gain a broader understanding of commitments (known as pegged requirements), such
as capacities, materials, and PRTs.
When you’re deciding which S&OP option (standard S&OP or flexible planning) to use,
it makes sense for you to evaluate business requirements and at the same time strive
to maintain simplicity and a straightforward approach to business processes. As men-
tioned already, standard S&OP offers a very limited set of functions, but being almost
completely predefined, it enables you to immediately start taking advantage of those
functionalities without any configuration. In general, we recommend that first, you
should try to cover your business process with standard S&OP, and then, if important
functions are still missing, you should consider using the rather complex and sophisti-
cated flexible planning tool instead. Flexible planning offers full functionality but
requires configuration, many presettings, and definitions.
SAP offers several standard analysis reports in all logistics areas, and they consist of
characteristics and key figures for a period. A characteristic value combination is the
combination of characteristic values with which a planner wants to plan. Characteris-
tics can be materials, plants, sales organizations, distribution channels, or purchase
organizations. Key figures (values or quantities) can be quantity produced, quantity
procured, operations quantity confirmed, production scrap (quantity), invoiced value,
or purchasing value. The period can be an interval, such as six months.
For example, standard analyses bring forth information such as the purchase and
invoiced values (key figures) of all the materials (characteristics) during the past six
months (the period). The standard analysis reports that SAP offers for each logistics
functional area have predefined information structures with no option to add new key
figures. However, you can create your own self-defined information structure in flexi-
ble planning by choosing the desired key figures from several available catalogs. A cat-
alog consists of a large number of characteristics of and key figures from a specific
application area, such as PP QM. Furthermore, you can even define how frequently the
system should update the values of key figures in flexible planning standard analysis.
Hence, standard analysis in flexible planning isn’t just applicable to the PP functional-
ity; the concepts and details we cover in this chapter are equally applicable to other
functionalities such as QM, PM, MM, and SAP S/4HANA Sales.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
548
Note
See Chapter 19 for more on reporting, including standard analyses in PP.
Our example of flexible planning from both perspectives (flexible planning and stan-
dard analysis in flexible planning) in this chapter remains primarily focused on SAP
S/4HANA Sales and its integral correlation with PP processes.
Note
Regardless of whether your company implements standard S&OP or flexible planning,
we encourage you to read the entire chapter because several features and functional-
ities are applicable to both planning types, and we eventually cover them all (not neces-
sarily in the same section). For example, we cover the forecasting functionality within
flexible planning, but it’s also available and can be used in standard S&OP for a material
or material group. We’ve also dedicated a separate section to forecasting. This is also
true for events and rough-cut planning. An event tends to have an impact on planning
figures, and a rough-cut planning profile provides better visibility into capacity, mate-
rial, and PRT situations. Similarly, we cover aggregation/disaggregation in standard S&
OP but not in flexible planning, although the option is available in both planning types.

11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning
Figure 11.1 shows that S&OP can include key figures from one of the following three
available options:
 Sales information system (SIS)
Planners use the SIS to take information from the sales history to propose a sales
plan.
 Profitability analysis
Planners use information from profitability analysis to help them make sales plans.
The system derives this information from sales and profit planning.
 Forecasting
Planners use historical data to come up with a sales plan. We cover forecasting in
Section 11.4.
The figures from S&OP are eventually transferred to demand management in the form
of PIRs, which form the basis of MRP.
We list the objects used in S&OP (which are standard S&OP and flexible planning) and
illustrate their logical relationships in Figure 11.2. The objects in S&OP form an integral
part in the planning process, so it’s important to have a comprehensive understanding
of them:
 Information structure
This is the data structure that stores important planning parameters. The planning
data is stored in key figures for the characteristic value combinations. As we previ-
ously explained, the characteristics can be the selection criteria based on which the
system brings up relevant key figures. Key figures can be quantities or values—for
example, the number of quality inspection lots, the total purchase value of raw
materials, operation quantities, or production quantities.
 Planning method
The storage, aggregation, and disaggregation of data with regard to the planning level
occur either as consistent planning or as level-by-level planning. In S&OP, the system
looks for the planning method that is defined in the information structure.
 Planning hierarchy
The planning hierarchy contains the characteristic value combinations for the char-
acteristics of the information structure.
 Planning table
This is where the planner carries out actual and interactive planning.
 Planning type
The planning type defines the layout or format of the planning table.
See Figure 11.2 to see how each of the objects is linked in S&OP.

sections.title: Information Structures
sections.text:
11.1.2 Information Structures
To create or change information structures, you use menu path SAP IMG  Logistics
General  Logistics Information System (LIS)  Logistics Data Warehouse  Data Basis 
Information Structures  Maintain Custom Information Structures. Alternatively, you
can use Transaction MC21 to create information structures and Transaction MC22 to
change them.
Figure 11.4 shows Info Struct. S076, which is used in standard S&OP. The planning result
is stored for each characteristic value combination among the six key figures listed in
the figure. Values for other key figures, such as special production and sales orders,
can’t be stored in this (standard) information structure.
In standard S&OP, you can’t change many settings, but we’ll review them to help you
better understand the parameters used in standard S&OP for information structures
and key figures.
Figure 11.4 Standard SAP Information Structure S076

11 Sales and Operations Planning
552
To view or set parameters for information structures and key figures, follow menu path
SAP IMG  Logistics  Production  Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP)  Master Data 
Set Parameters for Info Structures and Key Figures, or use Transaction MC7F.
On the screen shown in Figure 11.5, double-click the information structure (Table) S076
or select the same and choose Details, which opens the Change View “Info structure
planning parameters”: Details screen. This area stipulates that the planning method
used in standard S&OP is level-by-level planning (denoted by I in the Planning method
field).
Figure 11.5 Planning Parameters of Information Structure
Choose the Key figure planning parameters folder on the left-hand side of the screen,
and the screen shown in Figure 11.6 will appear. The lower half lists the key figures avail-
able for standard information structure S076. Double-click the ABSAT field and a popup
will appear (see Figure 11.7). Here, you can control whether the key figure can be used for
forecasting, and you can determine the type of aggregation (summation) of the
entered data in the planning table.

sections.title: Planning Methods
sections.text:
11.1.3 Planning Methods
Near the beginning of this chapter, we mentioned that in standard S&OP, the configu-
ration and other settings are predefined, with limited options to customize parameters
to meet the business requirements. For example, in standard S&OP, the configuration
for information structure S076 is preset with characteristics and key figures, and so is
the planning table in which a planner maintains the planning figures. Planning is based
on either individual materials or groups of materials (i.e., product groups), in which a
planner can define proportional factors.
On the other hand, with flexible planning, a planner can set up self-defined informa-
tion structures with desired characteristics and key figures, and planning is based on
planning hierarchies. A planner can also set up self-defined planning tables and per-
form either level-by-level planning or consistent planning.
Table 11.1 compares the planning methods available in standard S&OP and flexible
planning.
With standard S&OP, a planner can only plan by using level-by-level planning (see
Table 11.2), whereas with flexible planning, a planner can use both level-by-level and as
consistent planning. In level-by-level planning, the planning data is maintained at the
specified level only. Because the system doesn’t automatically aggregate or disaggre-
gate planning data, the planner will have to manually perform these functions.
Table 11.2 provides a detailed comparison of consistent planning and level-by-level
planning. To use consistent planning, it’s important for the planner to define the plan-
ning hierarchies in the system. A planning hierarchy enables a planner to define pro-
portional factors for each characteristic. For example, if there are three sales organiza-
tions, then the planner can define how much (as a percentage) each sales organization
(a characteristic) will contribute to the overall planning hierarchy. Similarly, changes
made to any planning level automatically update and account for changes in other
planning levels. For example, say that initially, a planner defined 10 materials, each of

which constituted a 10% proportion of the planning results. Now, if a new material is
added, the system automatically updates the proportional factor (100% ÷ 11 materials =

aggregates (divides up) the planning data to present a consistent planning position in
the planning table.

sections.title: Planning Types in Standard Sales and Operations Planning
sections.text:
11.1.4 Planning Types in Standard Sales and Operations Planning
The planning type defines the layout or the format of the planning table and thus rep-
resents the link between the planning table (where the actual planning is carried out)
and the information structure (in which the planning data is stored). It’s possible to cre-
ate several planning types for one information structure. For example, two different
planning types may contain planning data for two seasons (autumn and winter). The
data is stored in the information structure, so the different planning types depend on
each other because they use the same set of data. If you use level-by-level planning,
then it’s important to create a separate planning type for each planning level.
Standard S&OP has been configured with three planning types, which are used auto-
matically in standard S&OP planning:
 SOPKAPA
Planning type for the planning of individual product groups
 SOPKAPAM
Planning type for the planning of individual materials
 SOPDIS
Planning type for dual-level planning of product group hierarchies
In the following sections, we’ll go through the steps you need to perform to create a
product group that you can use in standard S&OP. We’ll also show you how to transfer
the planning results of a product group to demand management as PIRs.
Creating a Product Group
Figure 11.8 shows that in this example, we’re using information structure S076 in stan-
dard S&OP to create a product group called TURBINE_SOP for plant 1710. This product

group has two materials, 2262 and 2264, with proportional factors of 60% and 40%,
respectively. We explained aggregation and disaggregation earlier, but the propor-
tional factor is specific to level-by-level planning only and can either be maintained
manually or proposed automatically by the system.
To create the product group in the system, you follow menu path Logistics  Production 
SOP  Product Group  Create or use Transaction MC84. For Product group TURBINE_SOP
and Plant 1710, enter materials “2262” and “2264” and their proportional factors, “60” and
“40” (their percentages).
Figure 11.8 Creating Product Group Turbine_SOP
Then, choose the Product Group Graphic button to view the product group graphically
(see Figure 11.9).
Figure 11.9 Graphical View of Product Group

In addition to using interactive entry, you can calculate proportional factors on the
basis of historical data by following menu path Edit  Calculate Proportional Factors.
Use this option to see how well the self-defined (manual) proportional factors compare
with what the system proposes. That may help you prepare a realistic plan. Moreover,
you can equally distribute the proportional factors via menu path Edit  Distribute Pro-
portional Factors. Then, save the product group.
Creating a Plan for the Product Group
To plan the product group in standard S&OP, you use menu path Logistics  Production 
SOP  Planning  For Product Group  Create or use Transaction MC81.
Figure 11.10 shows the initial screen of the rough-cut plan for the product. Enter Product
group “TURBINE_SOP” and Plant “1710” and then press (Enter) to go to the Define Ver-
sion dialog box. On this screen, enter planning Version “A00” and a version descrip-
tion. Then, you again press (Enter) to go to the Create Rough-Cut Plan screen.
Figure 11.10 Planning in Standard S&OP
If the historical key figure data is already available in the system, then you can import
it to create a sales plan. Alternatively, you can manually enter the Sales quantities in
the planning table, as we’ve done in this example, by entering sales data for the next
nine months. On the screen shown in Figure 11.11, choose the More dropdown list to see
the following options for creating a sales plan for the product group (or for an individ-
ual material, where applicable):
 Transfer Plan from SIS
 Transfer CO-PA Plan
 Forecast
 Transfer Product Group Proportional from Production or from Sales
To use any of these options, the system must have significant historical data to help
with effective and reliable planning.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
558
Figure 11.11 Sales Plan in Standard S&OP
Options for Creating a Production Plan Automatically
Remain in the planning table (Transaction MC81). Figure 11.12 shows how to create a
production plan with the following options by choosing More from the top menu bar:
 Synchronous to Sales
Sales figures are used as operations plans.
 Target Stock Level
The operations plan is configured in such a way that the target stock level is reached
in each period.
 Target Days’ Supply
The operations plan is configured in such a way that the target days’ supply is
reached in each period.
 Stock Level = Zero
The operations plan is configured in such a way that the entire stock level is con-
sumed.
Depending on the business requirement, you can choose whether the production plan
should be equal to the target stock level or whether the target stock should be zero at
the end of the period. For this example, you create the production plan as being syn-
chronous with the sales plan. This production plan also takes the target stock levels
into account at the end of each period.
It’s important to note that these production figures have no direct implications for pro-
duction or procurement. The purpose of entering or incorporating these numbers
(which are key figures, such as sales or production) is to come to a common under-
standing, wherein relevant stakeholders, be they from sales or production, can transfer
the finalized key figures to demand management in the form of PIRs. For example, say
several planning versions are created in the system, from which input and feedback
© 

559
11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning
from sales and production are taken. The sales or production figures are then revised
either in the same planning version or after you create a new version. The finalized ver-
sion eventually transfers to demand management and is reflected as PIRs with the
Active box checked.
Figure 11.12 Options for Creating Production Plan in Standard S&OP
The next step is to transfer the production plan to demand management, where the
requirements of individual members (materials) of the product group are reflected as
PIRs. When the transfer of the product group quantities to demand management takes
place, the system disaggregates them at the material level according to the proportional
factor defined previously. In this example, proportional factors of 60% and 40% are
maintained for the two materials. The next section will show how the system disaggre-
gates (divides up) the total planning quantities of the product group in a 60 to 40 ratio.
Transferring to Demand Management
You can directly transfer the planning figures from the planning table to demand man-
agement by selecting the relevant key figures (e.g., production) and using menu path
Extras  Transfer to Demand Management. Alternatively, you can use menu path Logis-
tics  Production  SOP  Planning  For Product Group  Transfer PG to Demand Manage-
ment or Transaction MC75.
Figure 11.13 shows the initial screen where you assign Version “A00” of the product
group and plant for transfer to demand management. You select the Prod.Plan for Mat.
or PG Members as Proportion of PG radio button to transfer the production plan to
demand management.
Also, you must specify the requirement type and the version of PIR within which the
system transfers the values. In this example, enter “VSF” in Requirements type, enter
“00” in Version, and then choose Transfer now. A message log will then appear after the
transfer is made.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
560
Figure 11.13 Transferring Product Group to Demand Management
Planned Independent Requirements in Demand Management
To check and confirm whether the production figures of the product group were suc-
cessfully transferred in demand management as PIRs, you follow SAP menu path Logis-
tics  Production  Demand Management  Planned Independent Requirements  Display
or use Transaction MD63.
Figure 11.14 PIRs in Demand Management
©

11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning
On the initial screen, enter the selection parameters, such as Product Group “TURBINE_
SOP,” Plant “1710,” Requirements Type “VSF,” and Selected Version “00,” all of which
you previously defined on the screen shown in Figure 11.12. Then, press (Enter) and
you’ll see the screen shown in Figure 11.14, which shows how the product group breaks
down into individual members (materials), with the transferred quantity divided in a
ratio of 60% to 40%.

sections.title: Distributing Key Figures
sections.text:
11.1.5 Distributing Key Figures
During the course of planning the key figures, you often need to make mass changes to
several key figures at once. This can be tedious if done one by one or manually, so you
should use the Distribute functionality to make mass changes to a key figure in the
same row or column. The following are some of the changes that you can make:
 Perform mathematical calculations, such as addition, subtraction, multiplication,
and so on, on all values (or a selection of values) of a key figure.
 Increase or decrease all the values (or a selection of values) of a key figure by a
defined percentage.
 Distribute a value across the time series and replace the old values.
Figure 11.15 shows that you can distribute key figures from the menu bar by choosing
Distribute on the planning table. In the Distribution Functions dialog box that appears,
enter a number in the Val. Field and then select the right operand (OP). The list of avail-
able operands will then appear (and is shown on the right-hand side of Figure 11.15).
Figure 11.15 Distribution Functionality: Key Figures in S&OP

11 Sales and Operations Planning
562
Figure 11.16 shows a graphical representation of key figures—including sales, produc-
tion, and stock level—that gives materials planners the visibility they need into the
inward and outward movements of materials.
Figure 11.16 Graphical Representation of Key Figures in S&OP
The following sections cover aggregation and disaggregation, as well as tools and fea-
tures that are available not only in standard S&OP but also in flexible planning.
Aggregation and Disaggregation
During planning, available tools such as aggregation and disaggregation of key figures
help the planner to plan at one organizational (characteristics) level and transfer the
values (key figures) to other characteristics levels, based on the defined proportions.
Automatic aggregation and disaggregation are tools the planner can use to help pre-
pare a reliable and realistic sales (or production) plan. For example, say the planner
wants to consolidate (aggregate) the production quantities (key figures) at a plant level
(characteristic). In this scenario, the planner can use the aggregation tool to quickly
consolidate the desired information. Another example of aggregation occurs when the
sales organization is at the highest level, then the distribution channel level, then the
division level, then the material level, and finally the plant level.
In consistent planning, automatic aggregation and disaggregation are consistently
available at all planning levels, whereas in level-by-level planning, a planner needs to
manually trigger them. Because standard S&OP doesn’t offer consistent planning, a
planner only uses level-by-level planning in S&OP.
The system enables a user to drill down data from higher levels to lower levels (using
disaggregation) or progressively sum up the data from the lower levels to the higher
levels (using aggregation of the sum or average). The ultimate objective of planning,
either in standard S&OP or in flexible planning, is to create a plan at the lowest level of
© 

563
11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning
the characteristic combination (e.g., at the product group or material level) and forward
the plan to PP.
The planning hierarchy consists of characteristic value combinations and their disag-
gregation percentages. For example, if there’s a characteristic value combination of the
sales organization, distribution channel, division, material, plant, statistics currency,
and document currency, then a planner can view the historical sales data or a forecast
plan for given key figures of the characteristics. The system then disaggregates if the
data is viewed from the lower levels of the hierarchy, and it aggregates the same if the
data is viewed from the higher levels using the same set of disaggregation proportions.
Automatic Calculation of Proportional Factors
When the planning hierarchy is maintained, you use Transaction MC9B (Using Plan-
ning Hierarchy) for consistent planning to allow the system to calculate the propor-
tional factors based on the disaggregation proportions. These proportional factors
become the basis for aggregation and disaggregation in consistent planning.
Time-Based Disaggregation
When rough-cut S&OP transfers the data in demand management, the system requires
time-based disaggregation. In S&OP, planning is carried out in periods of weeks or
months, whereas the PIR needs a current date for its planning activities. If the business
process is simple, then the requirement of the period is scheduled for the first working
day of the period without any further configuration. However, if there’s a need for an
in-depth and complex distribution of quantities, then a planner will need to define dis-
tribution strategies and period splits. The planner assigns the distribution strategy to
the period split for weekly and monthly distribution, and the planner assigns the
period split, in turn, to the plant and the planning group.
Planning Hierarchy in Standard Sales and Operations Planning
The planning hierarchy in standard S&OP defines the proportion of one material with
respect to others. For example, having a material with a 40% proportional factor
enables the system to divide all planning figures of this material into 40% of the total
planning figures. You create planning hierarchy master data in standard S&OP by
maintaining product groups. You can also generate the master data automatically by
using Transaction MC8P for information structure S076. Alternatively, you can use
program RMCPSOPP (by using Transaction SE38) to generate the master data for infor-
mation structure S076. When you, as a planner, are in the product group as in standard
S&OP or are in a planning hierarchy in flexible planning, you need to define propor-
tional factors. The system then uses these defined proportional factors either in level-
by-level planning or in consistent planning.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
564
Table 11.1 provides an overview of the usage of proportional factors in consistent plan-
ning and level-by-level planning.
To aggregate or disaggregate planning figures in standard S&OP, you follow menu path
Logistics  Production  SOP  Disaggregation  Break Down PG Plan or use Transaction
MC76. Once you select the product group, plant, and version, the system will redirect
you to a screen where you can make changes to the proportional factor of each mem-
ber of the product group and where you can check the relevant boxes to run the stan-
dard macros when the planning table screen for standard S&OP appears.

sections.title: Working with Macros
sections.text:
11.1.6 Working with Macros
Macros enable you to automate the linking of key figures in the planning table by using
calculation rules. In standard S&OP, the system provides macros for the calculation of
the projected stock level and for creating an operations plan on the basis of the sales
plan. You can also use macros in aggregation and disaggregation during level-by-level
planning. However, you can’t define your own macros in standard S&OP.
In the planning table, you first select the Sales line item and then choose Macro, which
brings up the list of standard macros available to select from. For this example, choose
Aggregation: Sales, which aggregates the individual sales figures for a material.
Note
Self-defined macros in flexible planning are covered in greater detail in Section 11.2.6.

sections.title: Flexible Planning
sections.text:
11.2 Flexible Planning
It’s amply evident that standard S&OP offers limited options in terms of solutions to
diverse and complex business scenarios and processes. If your company intends to carry
out very lean and production-focused forecasting of sales figures, then we recommend
that you check whether standard S&OP can fulfill your business needs.

11.2 Flexible Planning
In more complex planning scenarios, flexible planning can help. In flexible planning, a
planner can plan and process planning data from any organizational unit, whether it’s
a sales organization, a distribution channel, or a production plant. Flexible planning
begins with designing an information structure, which is a structure with organiza-
tional units and business planning values—for example, forecast values in different
periods, their relationships, and different views of planning values from the perspec-
tives of different organizational units (e.g., sales, production, procurement). To success-
fully implement flexible planning, it’s very important for a planner to gain an in-depth
understanding of how to define an information structure to fulfill business planning
requirements.
You can also use information structures for product allocation. For example, in the
automobile industry, it’s common to assign sales quotas to different regions and auto-
motive dealers to ensure that a company’s automobile is sold in every region the com-
pany serves. In an information structure for production allocation in this example, you
would maintain a dealer-wise sales quota. During sales order creation for a specific
dealer, the system would then check the information maintained in the information
structure for product allocation to see whether any given dealer is still eligible to place
orders. And similar to standard S&OP, in which everything is preconfigured, in flexible
planning, you would design a table by using the basic steps of configuring a self-
defined information structure, setting the parameters for the information structure
and key figures, and configuring the planning type.
This section will show you not only how to successfully set up flexible planning but also
how to specifically cover the update fields (key figures) involved in flexible planning.
These update fields enable you to better use the flexible planning functionality when
features such as macros, aggregation or disaggregation, events, and rough-cut planning
aren’t involved. The update fields apply to standard analysis in flexible planning.
S&OP can create a planning hierarchy and maintenance of disaggregation percentages
for the characteristic value combinations in a hierarchy. As we mentioned previously,
a planning hierarchy is available in flexible planning only.
The section also covers self-defined macros, aggregation and disaggregation, events, and
rough-cut planning profiles. All of these features hold significant value for planners.

sections.title: Creating a Self-Defined Information structure
sections.text:
11.2.1 Creating a Self-Defined Information structure
Because we already covered the salient features of the information structure for flexi-
ble planning in Section 11.1.2, we’ll now create the self-defined information structure in
flexible planning.
To create and change these structures, you follow menu path SAP IMG  General Logis-
tics  LIS  Logistics Data Warehouse  Data Basis  Information Structures  Maintain Cus-
tom Information Structures. Alternatively, you can use Transaction MC21 to create
information structures and Transaction MC22 to change them.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
566
Figure 11.17 shows the initial screen where you can create an information structure. For
this example, you define the Info Structure as “S655” and enter the description as “SOP:
Info_Structure_Turbine.” It’s important to select the application because the resulting
catalog (consisting of characteristics and key figures) will then appear accordingly. For
this example, enter “01” as the Application for SAP S/4HANA Sales. It’s also important
to check the Planning Possible box to enable the user to perform planning functions in
flexible planning.
Figure 11.17 Self-Defined Information Structure Creation in Flexible Planning
Then, press (Enter) and the screen shown in Figure 11.18 will appear. Next, click the
Choose Chars button, which will bring up the option to select characteristics from the
catalogs. For this example, select the following characteristics from three catalogs:
 SD: Sales Organization (order)
– Sales Organization
– Distribution Channel
– Division
 SD: Article (order)
– Material
– Plant
 SD: Units (delivery)
– Document Currency
– Local Currency
If the selections are made in the correct order, the resulting screen will look as shown
in Figure 11.18.
© 

567
11.2 Flexible Planning
Figure 11.18 Characteristics and Key Figures in Flexible Planning
Next, to select the key figures, choose Choose Key Figures and then select the key fig-
ures from the following catalog. For this example, select the following key figures from
two different catalogs:
 Key Figures: SOP
– Sales
– Production
– Stock Level
– Target Stock Level
– Available Capacity
 SD: Values, Prices (Order)
– Net Price
– Net Value
All the key figures that are selected in Figure 11.18 are then copied to a temporary area,
and you confirm the selection by choosing Copy + close. If you’ve made the selections
in the correct order, then the resulting screen will look like the Key Figures area in
Figure 11.19.
Next, choose the Generate button to open the Log screen shown in Figure 11.20. Here,
information structure S655 is successfully created in the form of an SAP table. If there
are any errors, a warning or error message will appear, thus enabling you to address the
problem to avoid issues during the planning activity phase.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
568
Figure 11.19 Characteristics and Key Figures in Flexible S&OP
Figure 11.20 Information Structure in Flexible S&OP
© 

569
11.2 Flexible Planning
The next step in creating the information structure for flexible planning is to define
the planning method. The SAP S/4HANA system offers two types of planning methods,
as we’ve already discussed:
 Consistent planning
 Level-by-level planning
The planning method determines the method of disaggregation of data and the storage
of data at various levels of planning. Refer to Section 11.1.5 for details.
To set up a planning method for the self-defined information structure, you follow
menu path SAP IMG  Production  Sales & Operations Planning (SOP)  Master Data  Set
Parameters for Info Structures and Key Figures or use Transaction MC7F. On the over-
view screen that shows the various information structures that are available, double-
click information structure (Table) S655. The screen shown in Figure 11.21 will then
appear, wherein you enter “K” for the Planning method (for consistent planning) and
“1710” for the Planning Plant so that the system will automatically use this plant during
transfer of key figures to demand management.
Figure 11.21 Planning Parameters in Information Structure
Two other important fields are Statistics Currency and Base Unit of Measure. For this
example, you enter “USD” and “PC” (respectively) into these two fields. Entering “USD”
into the Statistics Currency field enables the system to use US dollars as a base currency,
whereas the Base Unit of Measure is the smallest unit of measure in which the system
will show inventory figures. If a material has a different base unit of measure than what
you’re using in planning, then you must define the conversion factor.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
570
You also enter “PRODU” into the Capacity figure field so that you can work with pro-
duction line values from the planning table while carrying out rough-cut capacity plan-
ning. Similarly, by entering “PRODU” into the Figure to Dem. Mgmt field (not shown),
you ensure that the system will use the production line values while transferring the
planned values to demand management.
Finally, you enter “US” into the Factory calendar field, which will enable the system to
refer to working days and holidays during rough-cut capacity planning.
Note
You can perform rough-cut capacity planning in a planning table to see whether the
planned forecast values are within the constraints—for example, available capacity at
a critical work center. If they aren’t, then you need to adjust the planned forecast val-
ues until the capacity load value is shown within permissible limits. For rough-cut
capacity planning, you use the production quantity from the planning table. The sys-
tem will then select the production quantities of different periods from the PP line in
the planning table to calculate the capacity requirement and display the capacity load
as a percentage value.
Next, select the Key figure planning parameters folder shown in Figure 11.21 and the
screen shown in Figure 11.22 will appear.
Figure 11.22 Key Figures in Information Structure
© 

571
11.2 Flexible Planning
The lower half of Figure 11.22 lists all the key figures you’ve selected during information
structure creation. Next, double-click ABSAT, and on the screen that appears, choose
between the following two options:
 Forecast
Check this box to allow forecasting. If you don’t check the Forecast box for the key
figures, then forecasting won’t be possible for the key figures in the planning table.
 Time-Based Disaggregation
This determines the type of aggregation with three possible options: the average
value of the total key figures value of a lower level is aggregated to the higher level;
the total of the key figure values of the lower level is aggregated to the higher level;
or no aggregation is to be carried out at all from the lower level to the higher level.
We recommend that you always use the total aggregation method.

sections.title: Planning Hierarchy
sections.text:
11.2.2 Planning Hierarchy
In standard S&OP, only level-by-level planning is possible. Although the characteristics
of the information structure already fine-tune the planning levels, a planner must still
assign characteristic values to them to carry out planning at those levels. For this
example, the custom information structure S655 contains the sales organization, dis-
tribution channel, division, material, plant, document currency, and statistics currency
characteristics. The purpose of a planning hierarchy is to enable the planner to define
the proportional factor of each of the characteristics and in the sequence in which they
are supposed to be planned. A planner creates a planning hierarchy for the following
important reasons:
 To let the SAP S/4HANA system know how the characteristic values are involved in
S&OP.
 To let the SAP S/4HANA system know the percentage proportional weight of each of
the characteristic values in the organizational hierarchy. This is known as the disag-
gregation percentage, which the system uses to disaggregate or aggregate the data
across various levels during planning and consolidation. We covered aggregation
and disaggregation in Section 11.1.5.
In level-by-level planning, you use the disaggregation percentage directly for disaggre-
gation/aggregation purposes. In consistent planning, you use the disaggregation per-
centages to calculate the proportional factors used in aggregation and disaggregation.
You must fulfill the following prerequisites to successfully create a planning hierarchy:
 Create an information structure with an active status.
 Configure the planning parameters for an information structure.
 Create the update rules and activate them (if applicable).
You can only create one planning hierarchy for a given information structure. In addi-
tion, note that you can only use one information structure in consistent planning,
whereas in level-by-level planning, you can use one or multiple information structures.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
572
To maintain the planning hierarchy for self-defined information structure S655, use SAP
menu path Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flexible Planning  Planning Hierarchy  Cre-
ate or use Transaction MC61.
Next, enter Info structure “S655” as shown in Figure 11.22. In the ensuing Characteristics
popup, enter all the details. Note that if the details aren’t maintained at a specific level,
the system triggers the planning hierarchy to maintain the proportional factor. For
example, if you don’t enter any details for Division, then the next three characteristics,
Material, Plant, and Document Currency, will enable you to reflect the planning hierar-
chy in which, for example, you can enter multiple material numbers with a proportional
factor for each.
Next, on the Create Planning Hierarchy: Statistics Currency screen, enter “USD” as the
Local Cur. with a Proportion of “100”, as shown in Figure 11.23.
Figure 11.23 Creation of Planning Hierarchy
Next, navigate from the higher level to the next level by selecting the characteristic val-
ues and choosing the Next Level icon (not shown) or by just double-clicking a character-
istic value. Also, check out the graphical representation by choosing the Hierarchy
Graphic button on the Maintain Planning Hierarchy screen or by using Transaction MC67.
Tips and Tricks
You can also use Transaction MC9A (Generate Master Data) to have the system auto-
matically create the planning hierarchy along with the disaggregation percentages
based on the historical data available in the information structure and its version. You
can also use the program to simulate the creation of a planning hierarchy.

sections.title: Planning Type
sections.text:
11.2.3 Planning Type
Once you have the planning hierarchy in place, the next step is to maintain the plan-
ning type, which then becomes an underlying template or layout in the planning table.
© 

573
11.2 Flexible Planning
As mentioned previously, in maintaining a planning type for flexible planning, a plan-
ner can define their own macros, assign events, allocate rough-cut capacity planning,
and deploy planning versions. The planning type then becomes a format, layout, or
template of the planning table, containing key figures and arranged in the desired way.
A planning type is similar to a Microsoft Excel program, in which a user can, for exam-
ple, include the headings on the left-hand side of the worksheet and the data for the
headings on the right-hand side of the worksheet.
A planner can also create multiple planning types for a given information structure.
Each planning type arranges the key figures of the information structure as desired on
a planning table that has its own macros to convert or translate the available data of an
information structure. Having multiple planning types enables the planner to plan the
same data arranged in different templates and from different perspectives to arrive at
a feasible operations plan. Alternatively, if a planner is going to use only one planning
type, then they should save the data under different planning versions and then final-
ize one final planning version, activate it, and transfer the finalized planning data to
demand management.
Note
In flexible planning, you can only transfer the finalized planning to demand manage-
ment if you have the plant characteristic and the material characteristic included in the
different characteristics defined in the planning hierarchy.
You create the planning types with reference to an information structure (in this exam-
ple, it’s S655). The name of the planning table must start with a Z or a Y only. The name
of the planning type can be up to 11 characters long.
To create a planning type, you use SAP menu path Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flex-
ible Planning  Tools  Planning Type/Macro  Create or use Transaction MC8A. On the
Planning Type: Create screen (see Figure 11.24), enter Planning Type “Z655,” check the
Line Layout and Macros boxes, and press (Enter).
Figure 11.24 Planning Type

11 Sales and Operations Planning
574
When the popup appears, enter Info structure “S655” and then press (Enter) again to
open the Define Planning Type dialog box (see Figure 11.25).
Figure 11.25 Creation of Planning Type
Next, define the planning horizon by entering the Planning start date, the Future peri-
ods involved in forecasting, and the Historical data that needs to be pulled in from the
information structure.
The planning start date is the date or the period from which you want to begin plan-
ning. This is the start of the future periods; that is, if you’ve opted for six future periods
starting on 07/01/2024 (July 01, 2024), and if you’ve set the period as monthly in the
configuration for the information structure, then the planning table will show six
monthly columns, starting with 07/2024 and running to 01/2025.
Similarly, if the historical data is set for six months, then the system will only pull in the his-
torical data for the past six months. This historical data will be used to forecast for future
periods. Press (Enter) or choose Continue to open the screen shown in Figure 11.26.
You can add key figures to the planning type by choosing the Structure button to add
the key figures, choosing the Add row button to insert a blank row, and choosing the
Actual data button to add a row that will bring into the planning table the actual data
that belongs to version A00 (which contains the actual data) of the information struc-
ture. To bring in the actual data for a specific key figure, first keep the cursor on that key
© 

575
11.2 Flexible Planning
figure and then choose the Actual data button. For example, if you place the cursor on
the Sales key figure and then choose the Actual data button, then the system will add a
row called Actual Sales below the Sales key figure.
Figure 11.26 Structure Assignment in Planning Type
You can’t edit the actual data row, although you can use it in formulas (macros) to
arrive at the data of other rows. You can use the actual data row to compare the actual
data to the planned data.
Figure 11.27 shows how the planning type will look after you successfully incorporate all
of the needed key figures into the planning table in the desired sequence. You will need
to repeatedly choose Structure to bring each key figure into the planning table in the
desired order or sequence. When you’re finished, save the planning type.
Figure 11.27 Planning Table in Flexible Planning

11 Sales and Operations Planning
576
Tips and Tricks
You can also bring in external data for a key figure by using the EXIT_SAPMMCP6_A00
user exit. To create a row for the external data, keep the cursor on a key figure and then
go to Edit  User Exit Data.

sections.title: Working with Self-Defined Macros in Flexible Planning
sections.text:
11.2.4 Working with Self-Defined Macros in Flexible Planning
Next, create self-defined macros in the planning type by using menu path Macro  Cre-
ate. Follow these steps:
1. In the Define Macro window, enter the Macro name and the Description of the macro
and then press (Enter).
2. Check the Display and Calc. selected area boxes on the screen shown in Figure 11.28.
3. Place the cursor on the first Operand field on the screen shown in Figure 11.28 and
then either use drilldown or press (F4) to select a key figure in the ensuing window.
For this example, select the key figure Sales and choose Continue.
Figure 11.28 Self-Defined Macro
4. Use the drilldown for the Op field, and on the screen in Figure 11.29, all of the normal
operands (addition, multiplication, etc.) will be available for you to choose from to
perform mathematical calculations.
5. Enter the value “0.90” in the second Operand field on the screen shown in Figure
11.28. Then, the calculated results (output) should appear as the Production key figure
in the Result field. Thereafter, whenever a planner manually enters values or makes
available the data in the Sales key figure of the planning table and executes the
ADJPROD macro, the Sales key figure will be multiplied by 0.9, and the results will be
shown in the Production key figure.
6. Save the macro.
© 

577
11.2 Flexible Planning
Figure 11.29 Available Operands
For the second example of using macros, you define a second macro, TOTAL, which
multiplies the Sales key figure by the Net Price to produce a Net Value key figure, as
shown in Figure 11.30.
Figure 11.30 Second Self-Defined Macro

11 Sales and Operations Planning
578
After manually entering the Sales quantity on the screen shown in Figure 11.31, you
simulate the self-defined macros by following menu path Macro  Execute, which
brings up the option to select a macro to execute. Execute both macros one by one and
then examine the results (see Figure 11.32) to see if they conform to the formulas
defined in the macros.
Figure 11.31 Executing Macros
Figure 11.32 Testing Self-Defined Macros
Tips and Tricks
You can record the macros by following menu path Macro  Record.
If you need to, you can delete obsolete or incorrectly created macros or edit them via
the same menu path within the planning type.
© 

579
11.2 Flexible Planning

sections.title: Row Attributes in a Planning Type
sections.text:
11.2.5 Row Attributes in a Planning Type
To set the properties or attributes of any key figure, you can simply double-click the key
figure or select it and choose the Row Attribute icon, as shown in Figure 11.33. You can
also mark a key field as a Planning line (input required), an Output line (display only), a
Text line (information-only key figure), or an Auxiliary line (which you can use as a vari-
able in macros). You can also determine the aggregation type of the key figure by
choosing the relevant radio button. In Figure 11.33, we changed the standard descrip-
tion of the Production key figure to Adjusted Production.
Figure 11.33 Line Attributes in Planning Type

sections.title: Planning in the Planning Table
sections.text:
11.2.6 Planning in the Planning Table
Apart from entering sales, calculating production quantities, and running macros, a
planner can perform the following functions (all of which we cover in this chapter, with
the exception of transfer to profitability analysis):
 Forecasting historical data in the planning table (see the Executing the Forecast sec-
tion ahead, as well as Section 11.1.4)
 Transferring to demand management (Section 11.1.4)
 Capacity planning (rough-cut planning; Section 11.1.5)
 Events (Section 11.1.6)
 Transferring to profitability analysis

11 Sales and Operations Planning
580
To create a new plan in flexible planning for the self-defined information structure,
you use menu path Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flexible Planning  Planning  Cre-
ate or use Transaction MC93. Follow these steps:
1. Enter the planning type as “S655” for this example. Alternatively, you can use “Z655”
for the planning type to clearly indicate a link with information structure Z655.
2. Enter the characteristics combinations for which to create the planning table.
3. Enter the Version number, “A00,” for the information structure data that is to be
used in the planning table. Planning version A00 is an active version.
4. Press (Enter) to open the planning table and then maintain the data from the infor-
mation structure for the key figures.
Instead of manually entering the Sales key figure, you can use the SIS, which we cov-
ered in Section 11.1.
In the following sections, we’ll cover how to execute a forecast, check and validate the
self-defined macros, and transfer the results of the forecast to demand management.
Executing the Forecast
First, select the Sales key figure row and execute the forecast from the menu in the
planning table by choosing Edit  Forecast.
Next, as shown in Figure 11.34, you select the Period intervals radio button and manually
maintain both Forecast and Historical data.
Figure 11.34 Executing Forecast in Flexible Planning
© 

581
11.2 Flexible Planning
For this example, you use historical data from the past six months to execute the fore-
cast for the next six months. Alternatively, you can maintain the same details by
choosing No. of periods and entering data into No. of forecast periods and No. of histor-
ical values.
Next, in the Forecast execution area, you select the forecast model, such as Seasonal
models. You can change or update the historical values by choosing the Historical but-
ton. A planner also can manually enter or correct the historical values, as we’ve done in
this example. When choosing the Forecasting button, depending on the forecast model
you’ve selected, you may need to enter further details, such as the Alpha Factor and
Gamma Factor (see Figure 11.35).
Figure 11.35 Additional Forecast Parameters
Tips and Tricks
To save yourself the work of manually entering all of the preceding details, you can use
the Forecast Profile button shown in Figure 11.34. We discuss creating forecast profiles
in Section 11.4.3.
You execute the forecast by choosing the Forecasting button on the screen shown in
Figure 11.35. This brings up the Forecast: Results screen, which shows the forecast values
for the next few months (see Figure 11.36). Choose Continue and all of the forecast fig-
ures will automatically be copied to the Sales key figure on the planning table.
The result is a graphical comparison of the historical and forecast figures for the period
under consideration—that is, the defined months of historical figures and forecast fig-
ures.
Note
Because we’ve separately covered forecasting in sufficient detail in this chapter, we’ve
intentionally limited the discussion here to only the basic steps involved in forecasting
in the planning table.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
582
Figure 11.36 Results of Forecast
Validating Self-Defined Macros
After you’ve put the Sales figures in place from the forecasting activity, the next step is
to manually enter the Net Price and then execute both of the macros (see Figure 11.37).
The system will then automatically fill in the Net Value and Adjusted Production key
figures (see Figure 11.38).
Figure 11.37 Executing Macros
© 

583
11.2 Flexible Planning
Figure 11.38 Planning Figures after Executing Forecast and Running Macros
With the Adjusted Production figures also now in place in the planning table, let’s trans-
fer the same to demand management, in which the system will reflect the quantities of
the production key figures as PIRs.
Transferring Key Figures to Demand Management
Next, while remaining within the planning table, select the Production row and choose
Extras  Transfer to Demand Management.
Figure 11.39 Transfer of Planning Data to Demand Management

11 Sales and Operations Planning
584
Alternatively, you can follow menu path Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flexible Plan-
ning  Environment  Transfer Material to Demand Management or use Transaction
MC90 (see Figure 11.39).
Then, assign Version “A00” of Info structure “S655” for transfer to demand manage-
ment. The Key figure for this example is PRODU (production), and the transfer horizon
begins on 07/09/2024 (July 09, 2024).
Furthermore, you must specify the requirement type and the version of PIR within
which the system should transfer the values. For this example, assign Requirement
Type “VSF” and Version “00” and then choose the Transfer now button in the menu bar.
A message log will then appear after the transfer of key figures to demand management.
Transferring Results Validation in Demand Management
Next, to check and confirm whether the production figures were successfully trans-
ferred to demand management as PIRs, you follow SAP menu path Logistics  Produc-
tion  Demand Management  Planned Independent Requirements  Display or use
Transaction MD63.
On the initial screen that appears, enter the selection parameters, such as Material
“2264,” Plant “1710,” Requirements Type “VSF,” and Version “00,” all of which you previ-
ously defined on the screen shown in Figure 11.39. Then, press (Enter), and on the
screen shown in Figure 11.40, check whether the transferred quantities are complete
and correct. You can also check the correctness of the transferred data for a product
group by entering the relevant product group, which in this example is “TURBINE_
SOP,” in the initial parameters selection screen in Transaction MD63.
Figure 11.40 Validation of Transferred Planning Data as PIRs
This completes our review of the standard steps involved in flexible planning. Next, we
discuss a few salient features of the planning table, especially those related to the plan-
ning hierarchy.
© 

585

sections.title: Additional Features of Planning Tables
sections.text:
11.2.7 Additional Features of Planning Tables
The following list reviews some additional features related to planning tables:
 Checking header information
To check the header information, which shows the planning hierarchy levels, choose
menu path Settings  Choose Header Info in the planning table.
 Firming/unfirming key figure data
During consistent planning and to ensure that aggregation and disaggregation
won’t change the key figure data, you can firm or unfirm the key figure data. Follow
menu path Settings  Firming  Switch On. We recommend that you then follow this
sequence in the firming process:
– Activate fixing
– Change the value
– Deactivate fixing
You can also deactivate fixing by following menu path Settings  Firming  Unfirm
Period.
 Determining decimal places and rounding
To determine the number of decimal places and also allow rounding of key figures,
press (F8) or choose Settings  Places after Decimal Point.
 Copying key figure data from another information structure
To copy data for a key figure from another information structure, choose Extras 
Copy Data. The copy of the key figure data then goes from the source information
structure for a given period to the target information structure.
 Showing row totals
To show the row totals, choose Settings  Show Row Totals.
 Transferring all planning table data
To transfer the entire data of the planning table to Excel, choose Extra  Microsoft Excel.
 Moving up or down the planning hierarchy
Use the following options to move up or down the planning hierarchy within the
planning table:
– Member
– Owner
– Next Member
– Previous Member
– All Members
11.3 Version Management
In the information structure, the planning data is retained in versions. This enables the
planner to carry out simulations. A planner can assign any number to a version, and

11 Sales and Operations Planning
586
these remain inactive, with the exception of two specific versions with defined mean-
ings and functions: version 000 (which always contains actual data) and version A01
(which is the active version).
You can directly update all of the following in the info structure against the respective
version numbers:
 Every version that is created and for which data is stored in the planning table
 All of the values for each of the key figures
 Characteristic combinations that are either entered manually or evaluated by mac-
ros, events, or forecasts
You can also copy one version’s data to another version and delete obsolete or unwanted
versions, as we’ll discuss in the following sections.

sections.title: Copying a Version
sections.text:
11.3.1 Copying a Version
You can use different menu paths or transactions to copy one version to another,
depending on whether you’re using standard S&OP or flexible planning:
 In standard S&OP
Choose Logistics  Production  SOP  Planning  Version Management  Copy or use
Transaction MC78.
 In flexible planning
Choose Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flexible Planning  Planning  Version
Management  Copy or use Transaction MC8V.
Figure 11.41 Copy Planning Version
© 

587

sections.title: Forecasting
sections.text:
11.4 Forecasting
To copy the PIR details of the source version to the target version of an information
structure, you enter the values for the Version into the Source version and the Target
version areas and then choose Copy at the top of the screen (see Figure 11.41). In this
example, the values you enter in the Source version and Target version fields are “001”
and “A00,” respectively. Alternatively, you can maintain the date range (the Period) to
copy the PIR details of the source version to the target version.

sections.title: Deleting a Version
sections.text:
11.3.2 Deleting a Version
You can use different menu paths or transactions to delete planning versions, depend-
ing on whether you’re using standard S&OP or flexible planning:
 In standard S&OP
Choose Standard SOP: Logistics  Production  SOP  Planning  Version Management 
Delete or use Transaction MC78.
 In flexible planning
Choose Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flexible Planning  Planning  Version
Management  Delete or use Transaction MC8W.
To delete one or more versions of an information structure, check the relevant boxes
next to the version numbers and choose Delete at the top of the screen.

sections.title: Scheduling a Copy Version or a Delete Version
sections.text:
11.3.3 Scheduling a Copy Version or a Delete Version
To schedule a background job to delete or copy a planning version or activate an exist-
ing planning version, use Transaction MC8K. To schedule a copy from one target ver-
sion of one information structure to another information structure, use Transaction
MC8Q.
11.4 Forecasting
A forecast is a prediction of future requirements and is based on the past consumption
trend over a given period of time. These consumptions can be, for example, material
withdrawals against the production order, process orders, or even cost centers. When-
ever a new material is created in the system, the system automatically incorporates the
info in the Consumption tab in the Additional Data area of the material master. The
consumption of finished goods can be against the sales order. Hence, forecasting offers
enormous benefits for planning any material type—whether it’s a raw material, a con-
sumable, a packing material, a semifinished good, or even a finished good—based on
historical consumption figures. While each withdrawal or consumption updates the
information in the system, a planner can also manually enter the past consumption
figures for a material to help drive and facilitate forecasting.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
588
A detailed analysis of consumption figures provides a pattern for or other valuable
information on consumption patterns—that is, a constant consumption model, a
moving average model, or a seasonal trend model. Therefore, the system offers several
standard forecasting models to choose from. If a planner isn’t sure which forecast
model to select, then the system allows the planner to select Automatic Model Selec-
tion to facilitate forecasting. However, using this option has its disadvantages.
Warning
SAP doesn’t recommend using Automatic Model Selection for forecasting because it
eventually ends up incorporating one of the many forecast models (e.g., constant, mov-
ing average, seasonal) available in the material master. The SAP-recommended fore-
casting model may not be in line with the actual business scenario.
Tips and Tricks
When using the forecasting tool, you should take a couple of materials as test cases
and run the entire forecasting cycle. This forecasting cycle includes maintaining histor-
ical values, selecting the right forecasting model, and maintaining additional forecast-
ing parameters. Finally, you should execute the forecast and see if the forecast results
closely match what the system is expected to do. If it doesn’t, then consider evaluating
other available forecasting models or refining the forecasting parameters. After gain-
ing the necessary experience with a limited number of materials, you should replicate
the same steps for other similar materials. Be sure to periodically review and update
the forecasting parameters to ensure reliable material planning results.
This section covers the forecasting options available in S&OP (which are standard and
flexible planning), as well as the options that are available in the material master in
case you want to run forecasting on a consumable material or a packing material,
which isn’t part of S&OP. We’ll begin by covering the fields and options that are avail-
able in the forecasting view of the material master (e.g., forecast models, configuring
and using the forecast profile). Finally, we’ll cover the weighting group and splitting
indicator that can be used in forecasting.

sections.title: Forecasting View in the Material Master
sections.text:
11.4.1 Forecasting View in the Material Master
You can maintain the forecast parameters in the forecasting view of the material mas-
ter by using Transaction MM02. Figure 11.42 shows the Forecasting view of Material
2264 for Plant 1710. Details of all the important fields on the screen are given in the sub-
sequent sections of this chapter.
© 

589
11.4 Forecasting
Figure 11.42 Forecasting View in Material Master
When you run the forecast for a material-plant combination (Transaction MP30) or for
the entire plant (Transaction MP38), the system uses information from the material
master and offers no option to make changes to the forecast parameters.
Note
Navigate to the Forecast menu in SAP S/4HANA system via menu path Logistics  Pro-
duction  Production Planning  Materials Forecast.
You can also execute forecasting in the following planning areas:
 S&OP for materials or for product groups
 Flexible planning
When you’re executing a forecast in either of these areas, the system provides you with
the options to select the forecast profile and make changes to the default data defined
in the forecast profile. You can also carry out forecasting for consumption-based mate-
rials, and essentially, the concepts and fundamentals remain the same.
Table 11.4 covers the fields shown in Figure 11.42.

590
Fields Descriptions

model N (no forecast/external model) enables the SAP S/4HANA
system to create forecasts from external sources, such as an
Excel file or even a legacy system. (See Section 11.4.2, in which
we cover this topic in detail.)

RefMatl: consumption This is the consumption data on a material that the system uses
to forecast values for a new material. If two or more materials
have similar consumption histories, then one material can form a
reference material for another, and yet, a multiplier can still
apply to an individual material to account for any increase or

definition of Multiplier below.)
Date to This is the date up to which the system uses the consumption
values of the reference material to calculate new material fore-

RefPlant:consumption This is the plant of the reference material.
Multiplier This is the multiplication factor that the system uses for the ref-
erence material’s historical values to calculate new material fore-
cast values.
Hist. periods This indicates the number of historical values the system uses for
a material forecast.
Forecast periods This indicates the number of period splits for which the forecast
values should be created.
Periods per season This indicates the number of periods that belong to a season. Use
this option only if the forecast model is a seasonal forecast
model.
Initialization pds. This is the number of periods used for initialization. Initializing is
resetting the system’s settings, which can be automatically or
manually enabled. Initialization takes place when the material is
forecast for the first time or when there are structural changes to
a material’s time series, such as a change in a material’s forecast
model, a change in historical values, or a change in forecast
values.
Table 11.4 Parameter Details of Forecasting Screen in Material Master
©
