11.4 Forecasting
Fixed periods This is the number of periods for which the system won’t recalcu-
late the forecast values in the next forecast. This option helps you
prevent fluctuation in a forecast’s calculation and can help when
the production can no longer react to changed planning figures
within the planning horizon.
Model selection This specifies whether the system should check for the values for
a trend or seasonal fluctuation or for a trend and seasonal fluctu-
ation. You should set this indication if you want the system to
help with automatic forecast model selection.
Optimization level This specifies the increment (rough, middle, or finer) by which
the system will optimize the forecast parameters.
Alpha factor This specifies the smoothing factor for updating the basic values.
If you leave this field blank, the default value will be 0.2.
Gamma factor (not
shown)
This specifies the smoothing factor for updating the seasonal
index. If you leave this field blank, the default value will be 0.3.
Tracking limit This specifies the amount by which (or the number of times) the
forecast values may deviate from the actual values. Every time a
forecast runs, the system compares the tracking limit with the
tracking signal. The tracking signal, which is calculated internally,
is the quotient and the mean average deviation. The system uses
the tracking signal to control the accuracy of the forecasts. If the
tracking signal is greater than the tracking limit, then the system
issues an exception message and asks the planner to (re)check
the selected forecast model.
Selection procedure This defines the procedure by which the system selects the opti-
mum forecast model. In the standard system, there are two pro-
cedures that the system uses:
 1: This uses a significant test to determine whether a trend or
a seasonal pattern is present, and then, it selects the forecast
model based on the results.
 2: This uses more precise and granular calculation than proce-
dure 1 but consumes more system resources. This procedure
carries out the forecast by using all models, optimizes the
parameters, and then selects the model with the smallest
mean average deviation.
Weighting group This indicates how many historical values are used to calculate
the forecast and what weighting is given to each of the historical
values.
Fields Descriptions
Table 11.4 Parameter Details of Forecasting Screen in Material Master (Cont.)

11 Sales and Operations Planning
592
Tips and Tricks
When you implement SAP S/4HANA is implemented for the first time, there aren’t any
historical consumption values available in the system that can form the basis of fore-
casting. In such scenarios, you can manually enter the unplanned or total consumption
quantities of a material. When you’re maintaining consumption values for a large
number of materials, creating an upload program is a more practical approach. To
manually enter the total consumption values into the material master (Transaction
MM02), select the Additional Data button and then choose the Consumption tab. Also,
define the plant for which the consumption values are maintained and for which fore-
casting is being executed.

sections.title: Forecast Models
sections.text:
11.4.2 Forecast Models
The purpose of forecast modeling is to aid in forecasting particular events. The forecast
model is designed around factors that the client believes are important in influencing
the future use of a material. The client also uses its past consumption of a material to
determine the material’s future use. Both of these methods should produce a reliable
forecast. A number of forecast models are available within SAP S/4HANA, as follows:
Beta factor This specifies the smoothing factor for the trend value. If you
leave this field blank, then the default value will be 0.1.
Delta Factor (not shown) This specifies the smoothing factor for the mean average devia-
tion. If you leave this field blank, then the default value will be
0.3.
Reset automatically If you check this box, then the system automatically resets the
forecast model if the tracking limit is exceeded. The system also
selects a new model during the next forecast run.
Param.optimization If you check this box, then the system optimizes the smoothing
factors needed by the given forecast model. Also, parameter opti-
mization is carried out for both the first forecast and subsequent
forecasts. The system calculates a number of different parameter
combinations and selects the one that produces the lowest mean
average deviation.
Correction factors If you check this box, the system calculates the forecast values by
taking into account the correction factors, as defined in the con-
figuration.
Fields Descriptions
Table 11.4 Parameter Details of Forecasting Screen in Material Master (Cont.)
© 

593
11.4 Forecasting
 Constant model
The constant model assumes that the use of material is constant. This isn’t to say
that the use of material is the same each month but that the variation in material
usage fluctuates little and a constant mean value has been calculated. This forecast
model can apply to electricity consumption in an office, for example. Although elec-
tricity consumption increases in summer months due to increased air conditioning
use, the consumption doesn’t vary a great deal from the mean value over a longer
period of time, such as a year.
 Trend model
The trend model is used when there is an identifiable increase or decrease in use of
a material over a period of time. The trend may include areas of movement away
from the trend, but the overall movement follows the trend. For example, a down-
ward trend over time may represent the use of printer cartridges for top-selling
printers that become obsolete over a short period of time, perhaps only 12 to 18
months. As the purchase and use of such printers decrease, the cartridges used in
such printers will also slow.
 Seasonal model
The seasonal model affects many businesses due to the weather, holidays, and vaca-
tions. The seasonal model is defined as a pattern that repeats during each period. For
example, the annual seasonal pattern has a cycle that is 12 periods long, if the peri-
ods are months. A seasonal model may be applicable to a company that makes patio
furniture, which experiences a greater demand in the months of May through Sep-
tember, and this pattern is repeated each year.
 Seasonal trend model
The seasonal trend model is similar to the seasonal model, except that instead of the
same pattern occurring each period, the pattern is moving further away from the
mean value, either positively or negatively. For example, California sparkling wine
manufacturers can see a positive seasonal trend. They have a seasonal pattern in
demand for their products, and for them, the seasonal pattern has a positive trend,
as sales have continued to rise. A negative seasonal trend can be shown for beer
manufacturers, who have a seasonal market, but the overall trend continues to be
negative as sales slow each year.
Tips and Tricks
If a client hasn’t developed forecast models in the past or doesn’t know which forecast
model fits best, it may want to allow the SAP S/4HANA system to analyze the historical
data and determine an appropriate forecast model to use. Forecast model J (automatic
model selection) is available in SAP S/4HANA. After the system selects a model, the cli-
ent can use it as a starting point and make modifications in the future. However, it’s
equally important to revisit the system-proposed-and-selected forecast model to
ensure that it closely matches the consumption trend.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
594

sections.title: Forecast Profile
sections.text:
11.4.3 Forecast Profile
A forecast profile consists of user-defined values (parameters), which then become avail-
able during the execution of forecasting. You can, however, still make changes to these
values (parameters) in the forecast profile before executing the forecast. To create a fore-
cast profile, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Sales & Operations Planning (SOP) 
Functions  Forecasting  Maintain Forecasting Profiles or use Transaction MC96.
Figure 11.43 and Figure 11.44 show a standard available forecast profile, SAP, which you
can copy to create a new profile and make desired changes.
Figure 11.43 SAP Forecast Profile
Details of the important fields in the forecast profile are given in the following list:
 Frm Period/to Period
This denotes the period or intervals for the historical data and forecast data that
you’ll use during forecasting.
 Forecast strat.
This is the forecasting strategies/models that the system will use to carry out the
forecast. Forecasting strategies are covered in Section 11.4.4.
 Period Ind.
This is the period or interval of the required future periods the forecasting will trigger.
© 

595
11.4 Forecasting
Figure 11.44 SAP Forecast Profile: More Options
 Periods/Season
This is the number of periods in a season if there is a seasonal trend.
 Factory Calend.
The factory calendars specify holidays or the fiscal year variant. For this example,
you assign factory calendar “US.”
 Max.hist.values
This is the maximum number of historical periods. For example, if you enter “12,”
the system will consider the past 12 months of historical values to suggest forecast
values.
 Consumpt.
You use this option to forecast consumption, as in standard S&OP’s level-by-level
planning forecast. It is based on the material’s consumption history.
 Forecast parameters
This corrects historical values for the following (see Figure 11.44):
– Alpha factor: This is used to smooth the basic value.
– Beta factor: This is used to smooth the trend value.
– Gamma factor: This is used to smooth the seasonal index.
– Sigma factor: This is used to correct the historical figures that lie outside the pre-
defined upper and lower tolerances. The lower the sigma factor, the greater the
control of forecast figures. SAP recommends using a value between 0.6 and 2.0.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
596

sections.title: Using the Forecast Profile
sections.text:
11.4.5 Using the Forecast Profile
If you select Forecast Profile while executing the forecast, the Forecast: Forecast Profile
popup will appear, and in it, you can select the relevant forecast profile. The system con-
siders all of the forecast parameters during forecasting. If you still need to make any last-
minute changes to the values defined in the forecast profile, then choose the Change
option to make the desired changes and execute the forecast. To configure a new forecast
profile, use Transaction MP80. We already covered how to execute a forecast in Section
11.2.6, so we’ll just summarize some of the more important topics of forecasting here.
When you execute a forecast, the system calculates the mean average deviation, the trend
value, and the error total of the forecast values. The mean average deviation option helps
you identify the degree of variability and make a subsequent decision on buffering strate-
gies to avoid stock-outs. For example, the system divides an error total of 44 by the mean
average deviation value of 13 (44 ÷ 13 = 3.385) and compares it with the tracking limit set at
4.000 (in the material master) to decide whether the forecast model is still applicable or
whether the situation warrants selecting a new or different forecast model. The error total
is the sum of all errors in the past consumption series (refer to Figure 11.36).
Tips and Tricks
When you’re executing a forecast, be sure to check out the forecast error messages by
double-clicking them and attending to them as needed.
The graphical versions of the forecast figures show the following:
 In yellow, the details of the historical corrected consumption values
 In blue, the details of the historical originating consumption values, if any
 In red, the originating forecasting values
 In green, the corrected forecasting values, if any
Tips and Tricks
The forecast model is another important parameter because it tells the system what to
look for: a trend pattern, constant consumption from period to period, or seasonality.
You can also choose to let the system perform a number of tests so that it can auto-
matically identify the pattern and set it after executing a forecast run.
Here are three different ways you can perform forecasting on individual materials or a
group of materials:
 At a single material and plant level, use Transaction MP30.
 At the total materials and plant level, use Transaction MP38.
 At a single material and plant level, use Transaction MM02.
© 

599
11.4 Forecasting
Tips and Tricks
To view or print the consumption values and forecast values of individual materials or
all materials in a plant, here are the transaction codes:
 Individual material: Transaction MP32
 Total forecast: Transaction MPDR

sections.title: Weighting Group
sections.text:
11.4.6 Weighting Group
The weighting group defines how many of the consumption values the system takes
into account during the forecast run and what weight to give to each value. Often,
recent consumption values get higher weights than past values.
The weighting group works only for the weighted moving average forecast model or
forecast model W. You can also use this forecast model to set up the delta factor to
smooth out the forecast values. To set up a weighting group, follow menu path SAP IMG 
Materials Management  Forecast  Weighting Groups for Weighted Moving Average.

sections.title: Splitting Indicator
sections.text:
11.4.7 Splitting Indicator
In forecast-based planning, it’s mandatory to execute material forecasts first. Based on
the results of the forecast, the system calculates and plans future consumption quanti-
ties. You can select the forecasting periods to be days, weeks, months, or entire years.
The system can consider safety stock in the net requirement calculations. The system
also takes receipts into account to ensure the demand from the forecast is met. If the
demand isn’t met, then the system creates procurement proposals.
You can specify the number of historical periods that the system will use in forecasting,
and you can also specify the future forecast horizon (periods) for each material. After
the planning run, the system makes material available at the beginning of the period.
You have the option to further divide or split these material requirements into finer
detail by using the Splitting Indicator option in the MRP 3 view of the material master.
You can also separately define the period pattern for the forecast (day, week, month, or
posting period) and the number of prediction periods for each material. However, the
period pattern of the forecast may not be detailed enough for MRP because it may not
provide the required granularity. For this reason, you should use the Splitting Indicator
to define for each material how the forecast requirement values may further be distrib-
uted to a more detailed period pattern for MRP purposes.
You maintain the Splitting Indicator option in configuration by following menu path
SAP IMG  Materials Management  Forecast  Define Splitting of Forecast Requirements
for MRP. The splitting indicator contains the number of periods to be considered and
the number of days and/or weeks for which the splitting is to be carried out.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
600
The following are the three possible forecast requirements:
 No. of Days
This is the number of periods that are to be calculated to the day in MRP.
 No. of Weeks
This is the number of periods that are to be calculated to the week in MRP.
 No. of Periods
This is the number of periods—in addition to the periods to be split—for which the
system is to carry out the net requirements calculation in the planning run.
Note
As per SAP Note 2268100, SAP plans to replace the current material forecast solution in
the future. The future solution will perform the forecasting algorithm in SAP HANA by
utilizing SAP HANA’s forecasting library, thus improving material forecast perfor-
mance. In the meantime, you can still use the material forecast transactions, such as
Transaction MP30, as there is no functional equivalent yet in SAP S/4HANA.

sections.title: Rough-Cut Planning Profile
sections.text:
11.5 Rough-Cut Planning Profile
To carry out capacity analysis in standard S&OP or in flexible planning, you need to
maintain master data for the capacity requirements for production of a base quantity
of the material or product group. This master data is known as a task list, and it can be
a routing, rate routing, master recipe, or rough-cut planning profile. Normally, you cre-
ate a rough-cut planning profile for the material-plant combination because it provides
a rough estimate of the capacity requirements.
Warning
An important point to consider is that not every information structure created or used
in S&OP has a plant as a characteristic. This makes capacity planning very difficult. For
this reason, you should enter a planning plant into the Set Parameters for Info Struc-
ture and Key Figures configuration screen (Transaction MC7F). Also, without reference
to a plant and material, transfer of key figures to PIRs isn’t possible.
Because capacity evaluation is performed at the plant level, we advise you to include it
in the information structure and the percentage splits given to the information struc-
tures for disaggregation and aggregation purposes.
Also, on the Set Parameters for Info Structure and Key Figure screen (Transaction
MC7F), you enter a key figure in the Capacity Figure field; the numbers for this key fig-
ure in the planning table will be considered in capacity planning. For this example, we
used the PRODU value in the capacity figure (refer to Figure 11.21).
© 

601
11.5 Rough-Cut Planning Profile
Based on the details given in the rough-cut planning profile, the resource leveling cal-
culates the resource load for the operations plan and thus enables you to quickly esti-
mate feasibility in terms of capacity.
Next, we’ll cover the rough-cut planning profile and how the system reflects pegged
requirements.

sections.title: Creating a Profile
sections.text:
11.5.1 Creating a Profile
To create a rough-cut planning profile, use SAP menu path Logistics  Logistics Controlling 
Flexible Planning  Tools  Rough Cut Planning Profile  Create or use Transaction MC35.
On the initial screen of a rough-cut planning profile, enter Info structure “S655” and
choose Execute, which opens the popup shown on the right-hand side of Figure 11.46.
Enter all of the characteristic details and press (Enter), and the General Data screen
shown in Figure 11.47 will appear.
Figure 11.46 Initial Screen for Characteristic Assignments
On this screen, enter “22” in the Time span field and enter “1” PC in the Base quantity
field. Set the Status to “4” (for Released), set the Usage to “1” (for production), and then
press (Enter).
Tips and Tricks
You can change the parameters on the General Data screen when needed. For exam-
ple, you can change the number of working days to account for any national or unan-
nounced holiday(s) in a month.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
602
Figure 11.47 General Data in Rough-Cut Planning Profile
On the screen that appears, shown in Figure 11.48, double-click the first line of the
Resource Table column. Then, in the Resource Type dialog box, choose Work center.
The Resource popup will then appear. Enter “Turbine” for Work Center, “1710” for Plant,
and “HR” for Unit. Repeat the same steps for another work center, “assembly.” Also add
Material “2265” and a cost element to the rough-cut planning profile. Maintain the val-
ues as shown in Figure 11.49 and save the entries.
Figure 11.48 Rough-Cut Planning Profile
© 

603
11.5 Rough-Cut Planning Profile
Figure 11.49 Rough-Cut Planning Profile with Relevant Data
In Transaction MC94, the planning table displays the available capacity and capacity
requirements via menu path Views  Capacity Situation  Rough-Cut Planning  Show
(see Figure 11.50).
Figure 11.50 Rough-Cut Planning View in Planning

11 Sales and Operations Planning
604
In the Planning Table area, the system shows the capacity requirements against the
available capacity at the work center, based on the monthly production figures.
The system immediately calculates the capacity utilization when the plan changes, and
that facilitates the interactive adjustment of the operations plan to the capacities. How-
ever, the resource-leveling process doesn’t provide any standard function for adapting
the production plan to the available capacity.
If a capacity is overloaded, a corresponding message is displayed in the footer. For this
example, this is the case for 07/2024 (July 2024). There’s also a capacity overload for the
next several months, as shown in the blue font in the monthly columns in the Capacity
load row. If you scroll down in the same section, the capacity requirement details of the
second work center will also appear.
Use the same menu path—Views  Capacity Situation  Rough-Cut Planning  Show—to
view the costs and material values and choose the relevant option.

sections.title: Pegged Requirements
sections.text:
11.5.2 Pegged Requirements
Within the planning table, pegged requirements offer you a quick way to gain a better
understanding of a workload on a resource—whether it’s a work center, a material, or a
PRT—to evaluate pegging. The term pegging is synonymous with association or attach-
ment, which means one planning element is attached to another. To view the pegged
requirements within the planning table, you can choose Views  Pegged Requirements to
open the Source of requirements screen (see Figure 11.51). Alternatively, you can double-
click the Capacity Reqmt field shown in Figure 11.50 to make the screen shown in Figure
11.51 appear.
Figure 11.51 Pegged Requirements
© 

605

sections.title: Events
sections.text:
11.6 Events
11.6 Events
Major industry events, national holidays, sporting events, planned promotions, and
festivals can affect sales quantities (with increases or decreases), so understanding
events in advance will help you with planning efforts. You can increase or decrease the
sales quantities using a functionality called events.
The events functionality is applied to the sales key figures in all possible cases or to any
other key figures that give sales quantities or sales values. The system provides the
option to assign events across all characteristic levels or for a specific characteristic
level to which the event should be applied.
For example, let’s say that initially, a planner used the forecasting tool and seasonal
model to come up with planned sales figures. Later, the company decides to participate
in an important and relevant trade show that should have a positive impact on the
company’s sales. To attend to this anticipated surge in product sales (or demand), a
planner can incorporate this event (the trade show) into the planned sales figures to
enable the system to account for the increase. The events are assigned to the planning
types and thus come into play in the planning table.
Note
Creating an event isn’t a mandatory activity in the planning process, so a planner can
opt out of the event option in the planning situation, if needed.
The SAP S/4HANA system provides two ways of modeling an event: cumulative model-
ing and percentage modeling. For cumulative events, the sales quantity affected by the
event is maintained as a difference quantity in absolute figures. The overall sales quan-
tity is represented as the total of normal sales and includes the event. For percentage
events, the sales quantity affected by the event refers to the quantity planned in the
Sales key figure.
In the following sections, we’ll discuss how to create events and assign them to plan-
ning. We’ll also show how an event’s assignment impacts materials planning.

sections.title: Creating Events
sections.text:
11.6.1 Creating Events
To create events, you use menu path Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flexible Planning 
Tools  Events  Create or use Transaction MC64.
The initial screen on which to create an event is shown in Figure 11.52. For this example,
you enter the Event Number as “S655: SOP EVENT” and then press (Enter). In the General
Information popup, select the Proportional radio button and then press (Enter) again.
On the Proportional Event screen shown in Figure 11.53, enter the monthly Change in
percentage and then choose Assignment, which opens the Info Structure popup. In this
popup, assign the Info Structure as “S655.”

11 Sales and Operations Planning
606
Figure 11.52 Creation of Event
Figure 11.53 Assigning Event to Information Structure
Now that an event is created, the next step is to assign the event.

sections.title: Assigning Events
sections.text:
11.6.2 Assigning Events
When you’re done creating the event in the previous section, the screen shown in
Figure 11.54 appears. On this screen, the characteristics of the information structure
(such as Sales Organization and Division) automatically appear and are filled in, along
with the other information shown.
© 

607
11.6 Events
For this example, select the Sales key figure radio button and then select the A00 radio
button for the active version. Finally, the system will propose the current data for the
event, which you can change. Also, check the Status box to indicate that the event sta-
tus is active. Finally, choose Assignments, which provides the list of event assignments
that can be changed or even deleted (see Figure 11.55). Be sure to check the box located
on the far left-hand side of this event so as to ensure correct assignment with the infor-
mation structure.
Figure 11.54 Assignment of Event
Figure 11.55 List of Event Assignments

11 Sales and Operations Planning
608

sections.title: Events in Planning
sections.text:
11.6.3 Events in Planning
Now that you’ve made all the necessary settings and assignments for the event, let’s
test to see whether the planning results are reflective of the event in the planning type.
The planning type table is a frame designed to enable the planner to plan the planning
figures. Therefore, it makes sense to link the events in the planning type itself, so that
when the planning type is used to plan on a planning table (using the key figures), the
events come into play automatically.
Figure 11.56 shows the planning type change screen (Transaction MC8B) for Planning
type Z655 and Info structure S655.
For this example, select the Sales radio button and choose the Event button from the
menu bar so that the Stock level key figure appears for selection. Save the planning type.
Figure 11.56 Events in Planning Type
The next step is to change the existing plan (or create a new plan) for the same planning
type Z655 and information structure S655 and see how the entered stock figures reflect
the impact of an event (see Figure 11.57).
In this step, you use a key figure from the existing set of key figures to represent events
in the planning type. Normally, such a figure is an unused or unwanted key figure from
the list. If a company already knows that it’s going to use events in planning, we suggest
that you have one or two unwanted key figures that aren’t related to any planning
activities and that you’ll exclusively use for purposes such as an event. If all of the key
figures are already used in the planning type, then you can delete a key figure (by using
Transaction MC8B) and then use it to add the event so that when you click the Event
icon, the key figure will be shown as available for addition.
© 

609
11.6 Events
In the planning type, place your cursor on a key figure (in a location where a planner
wants the event-related key figures to appear—preferably below Actual Sales or Sales
Quantities) and then choose Event. This action shows a list of unused key figures from
which you can choose. Select one and press (Enter); the system will then draw in four
other new event-related key figures in its place. In short, the system will require you to
choose a key figure that can be overwritten with the four new key figures required for
planning events.
Figure 11.57 Events in Flexible Planning: Part 1
Figure 11.58 shows the change mode screen (Transaction MC94), on which you can manu-
ally enter quantities for each month in the Sales field. Notice that the Proportional Event ->
Sales field reflects the details of the event (event number: S655:SOP Event; not shown).
Figure 11.58 Events in Flexible Planning: Part 2

11 Sales and Operations Planning
610
Note
It’s very important to note that the impact of an event won’t be visible on the planning
table unless a planner makes changes to quantities in the Sales field and saves the
planning table. Then, the next time that a planner enters the planning table, the plan-
ning table values that are populated in the event-based key figures will appear. For this
example, those values are in the Corrected -> Sales field, which takes the percentage
change (proportional) from the Proportional Event -> Sales row.
Note
Refer to SAP Note 2380568 for details about the simplification item that changed the
scheduling parameters in configuration.

sections.title: Mass Processing in Sales and Operations Planning
sections.text:
11.7 Mass Processing in Sales and Operations Planning
As a planner, you must maintain a large and diverse range of materials and products
during the planning phase. In many planning steps, it’s crucial for you to perform the
requisite functions online and interactively; however, you can automate a few plan-
ning steps in the mass processing functionality of S&OP. Some of the mass processing
steps that you can perform are listed here:
 Forecasting
 Determining the opening stock level
 Running a macro
 Transferring planning data to demand management
 Copying key figure information from one information structure to another
 Transferring S&OP key figure data to profitability analysis
Because these steps eventually become routine work for a planner, it makes sense to
use the mass processing function.
In mass processing, you define activities that describe the individual steps that are car-
ried out, as well as their assignment to mass processing jobs. Before creating a mass
processing job in S&OP, you must create a planning activity.
The following sections will discuss planning activity and setting up and scheduling
mass processing jobs.

sections.title: Planning Activity
sections.text:
11.7.1 Planning Activity
A planning activity is a function that a planner will execute through a given mass
processing batch job. A planning activity consists of function profiles, which define
© 

611
11.7 Mass Processing in Sales and Operations Planning
functions in detail. A planner will need to create each of the function profiles separately
before creating the planning activity in SAP S/4HANA.
To create a planning activity, you use menu path SAP IMG  Production  Sales & Operations
Planning (SOP)  Functions  Mass Processing  Define Activities or use Transaction MC8T.
In the Key F.plng.activity field, enter the name of the planning activity (for this exam-
ple, “SOP”), the Planning Type (“Z655”), the planning activity type, and the correspond-
ing activity profile that needs to be executed through this planning activity. For a given
function that requires mass processing execution, choose the relevant activity type.
After you select the relevant activity, the corresponding activity profiles, (such as
Macro, Transfer Profile, and Copy Profile) become available to select from.

sections.title: Setting Up a Mass Processing Job
sections.text:
11.7.2 Setting Up a Mass Processing Job
To set up the mass processing job, you need to perform the following steps in the given
sequence:
1. Assign a name to the mass processing job.
2. Assign the information structure and version.
3. Assign the planning type.
4. Assign a name to the variant.
5. Select the selection screens.
6. Define the characteristic values, activity, and aggregation level.
7. Save the variant.
To create the mass processing job, follow SAP menu path Logistics  Logistics Control-
ling  Flexible Planning  Planning  Mass Processing  Create or use Transaction MC8D.
(Use Transaction MC8E to change the job and Transaction MC8F to delete it.)
Then, perform the following steps:
1. Create a planning job. For this example, enter the Job Number as “SOP_SAP” and a
short description of the Job Name as “SOP_SAP_Flex_Plan.”
2. Choose Execute and maintain Info Structure “S655” and Version “A00” to apply the
mass processing job to this information structure and version.
3. Choose Execute and maintain Plng.Type “Z655,” which is the specific planning type
within the information structure with which the system will perform the mass pro-
cessing function.
4. Select a planning type and then maintain the Variant Name for the selection screen
details.
5. In the Create Variant field, enter the variant name, which in this example is “SOP_
Flex_Plan.” Then, choose Create to create the variant and enter the selection screen
details.

1. Select the characteristic levels on which the system will run the mass processing job:
the Aggregation Level and the Planning Activity. You configured the planning activ-
ity in Section 11.7.1, and it consists of functions that the system performs through
mass processing. It also contains the profiles of the respective functions that are
configured.
2. Click the Attributes button to go to the Variant Attributes popup.
3. Check the relevant box to select whether to run the mass processing in the back-
ground only. Save the variant attributes.
11 Sales and Operations Planning
612
6. Check the Created box and choose Continue to proceed with maintaining the selec-
tion screen details.
After you have finished creating the planning job, perform the following functions:

sections.title: Scheduling the Mass Processing Job
sections.text:
11.7.3 Scheduling the Mass Processing Job
You can schedule the mass processing job by using Transaction MC8G. Here, you spec-
ify the variant name and use the standard batch scheduling function.
You can monitor mass processing jobs by using Transaction SM37. Use Transaction
MC8I to obtain an overview of a specific job. To delete a mass processing job, use Trans-
action MC8F.

sections.title: Standard Analysis in Flexible Planning
sections.text:
11.8 Standard Analysis in Flexible Planning
Now that we’ve discussed most of the options and features available in flexible plan-
ning, we’ll cover a lesser-known functionality of standard analysis in flexible planning.
In Chapter 20, we’ll discuss how to take advantage of the large number of standard
analysis reports that are available by using predefined and preconfigured information
structures in all the logistics areas of the SAP S/4HANA system. However, this section
covers a user-defined information structure (not a predefined information structure)
that can still be used in standard analysis. User-defined standard analysis helps when a
planner wants to evaluate all the desired key figures in one report (analysis report) by
using one information structure. This eliminates the need to refer to two or more pre-
defined information structures to obtain the desired information.
These are the steps you perform to create a standard analysis in flexible planning:
1. Maintain a self-defined information structure (with Transaction MC21).
2. Set parameters for the information structure and key figures (with Transaction
MC7F).
3. Create update rules for the information structure (with Transaction MC24). Maintain
an update rule for each key figure and then choose Activate Updating. You may also
use the Check icon to simulate update rules. Finally, choose the Generate icon to
generate the update rules for the information structure.
© 

613
11.8 Standard Analysis in Flexible Planning
In flexible planning, you update the rules for key figures within the information struc-
ture so that when you’re running the standard analysis report, the system will bring up
the relevant information.
The step in which you define the update rule occurs right after you create and activate
the information structure. Using Transaction MC24, you create the update rules for the
characteristics and key figures of the information structure.
The update rules enable the system to do the following:
 Know which characteristics and key figures it must update when the user performs
the business transactions.
 Know the SAP tables from which it needs to fetch the data for given key figures and
characteristics. They also include information for the dates the system should use
for records or data that is populated in the structures.
The update group is equally important because it defines the business areas and the
tables that will be used for updating the information structure. You can maintain
multiple update rules for a given key figure by copying an existing rule.
The SAP S/4HANA system offers functional areas such as materials management, SAP
S/4HANA Sales, production planning, quality management, project system, and plant
maintenance, among others.
Warning
To ensure that the system isn’t overburdened, you should only include the key figures
for which there is a need to update the information structure from SAP S/4HANA sys-
tem tables in the application area.
Note
To update groups in configuration, follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics Information
System (LIS)  Logistics Data Warehouse  Updating  Updating Definition  Specific
Definition Using Update Rules  Maintain Update Rules.
Before you complete the activation, check the consistency of the set update rules by
choosing the Generate button to check for any errors or warning messages and elimi-
nate them. The system won’t generate an information structure with errors, and no
data will be populated in such an information structure.
After you attend to the update rules, the next step is to configure the parameters to
update data in the information structure. The period split must match the period for
planning—that is, Set Planning Parameters for the Information structure (see Section
11.2.2). To configure the parameters, follow these steps:

11 Sales and Operations Planning
614
1. Perform the business transactions, such as creation of a sales order or production
order.
2. Create the necessary transaction data in the SAP S/4HANA system to test the update
of the information structure in the SAP tables. For example, you can create a sales
order with an order quantity and order value (if these two key figures are used in the
self-defined information structure). You can then see whether the data is automati-
cally updated in the information structure (table) by using Transactions SE16N or
SE11 and giving the details of the self-defined information structure.
3. Run a standard analysis of the self-defined information structure (Transaction
MC9C). On the initial screen, enter self-defined information structure “S655” and
version “A00” (actual data). Then, press (Enter), and on the next screen, the defined
characteristics will be available to choose from (see Figure 11.59).
Figure 11.59 Standard Analysis for Self-Defined Information Structure S655
Tips and Tricks
Check the update log by using Transaction MC30. Enter the user ID and choose Exe-
cute. Keep the cursor on the information structure number and choose the Details but-
ton to check the data updated in detail. Then, choose the Analysis button to check the
programs used in updating the information structure.
Note
All of the master data and transactions for flexible planning are listed in one place,
under menu path Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flexible Planning.
© 

615

sections.title: Summary
sections.text:
11.9 Summary
11.9 Summary
S&OP offers the potential for significant optimization of business processes by helping
the planner use standard S&OP or flexible planning. For standard S&OP, the planner
uses the product group to define the percentage or the proportional factor of each
material within the group or hierarchy, whereas in flexible planning, the planner uses
planning hierarchies.
A planner can extensively use standard or self-defined macros and also create large
numbers of planning versions. The final operations plan gets transferred to demand
management via PIRs. The forecasting tool is also available in standard S&OP and in
flexible planning to facilitate proposing realistic planning figures. Events help a plan-
ner take any specific spike or dip in planning figures into account, and the rough-cut
planning profile provides better visibility into capacities and materials commitments.
A planner can perform a large amount of routine planning work by using the mass pro-
cessing functionality, and they can also use flexible planning to perform standard analy-
ses for the self-defined information structure.
The next chapter covers demand management.
